user_ask_sources: instruction.md (Turn 1: the verbatim implementation plan delivered as the first user message)
user_simulation_prompt.md Turn 2 verbatim: 'seems to work please commit changes and open PR'
user_simulation_prompt.md Turn 3 (workflow context): the PR creation skill checklist text
_provenance.user_grounding_rule: Every goal MUST trace to a span the user actually wrote (Turn 1 plan body, Turn 2 commit/PR ask, or Turn 3 PR checklist). Implementation details that appear only in the oracle session, oracle_session.jsonl, README.md, or task-author scaffolding (test_manifest.yaml, tests/test.sh, reference_patch.json) are EXCLUDED.

explicitly_out_of_scope_charts: ModelTokensChart (instruction.md '## Charts NOT changed', marked already-fine)
user_verification_steps: Types and lint pass under the repo's verify script
On project- and developer-oriented charts, selecting a different metric reorders the legend but keeps per-series colors
On the error-oriented chart, switching between error metrics reorders the legend but keeps per-series colors
On the dimension-analysis chart, selecting a split dimension shows the legend sorted by highest split value first

completeness_goals.goal: Anti-effort floor (weight 0.0, gating-only). Reject the submission entirely if it exhibits any of the standard non-work patterns: (a) zero source-file edits (the diff is empty or touches no .tsx/.ts/.js source under apps/web/src/components/charts/); (b) git-operation-only patches that do not alter source content (e.g. solutions that run `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard`, `git revert`, `git stash`, or merely commit an unchanged tree); (c) a 'large' patch where the only material change is whitespace, reformatting, import reordering, or comment additions/edits — i.e. the AST of the named chart files is unchanged; (d) comment-only diffs that add TODO/FIXME notes describing the work instead of doing it; (e) whitespace-only diffs; (f) test-file deletion, .skip / .only insertion, or weakening of existing test assertions to make the verifier pass without implementing the asks; (g) deleting or stubbing out the named chart components, removing the metric selector, removing the dimension split control, hiding the legend, throwing inside render, or short-circuiting the data hook so the chart no longer mounts — these turn the legend-ordering and color-stability asks into vacuous successes. This floor contributes no positive credit; its only effect is to zero out solutions matching the above patterns.
completeness_goals.rationale: True anti-effort floor with weight 0.0. The user's plan is framed as a refinement on top of working charts ('Chart legends on the right side have two problems...') and assumes the charts render and switch metrics. Per the Gemini audit, an anti-effort floor must NOT contribute positive credit — otherwise an unmodified repository that happens to render correctly would receive 0.1 points for doing nothing. The floor's job is gating: zero out submissions matching the enumerated cheating patterns. The 0.1 weight freed from this goal has been redistributed across the user-traced implementation goals (goal_1..goal_6) proportional to their existing weight, preserving the project-vs-developer balance the user signaled in instruction.md.
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_notes: Standard anti-effort patterns enumerated in the goal body. Judges should apply this as a gate: if any pattern matches, the rubric score is 0 regardless of other goal verdicts. Surface tokens are not a substitute for a working surface; an empty diff is not a substitute for implementation; comment-only diffs are not a substitute for code; test weakening is not a substitute for correctness.

completeness_goals.goal: In apps/web/src/components/charts/ProjectTrendChart.tsx, color choices for each plotted series are pinned to series identity (the project) via the colorMap structure the user spelled out in the plan (a stable map keyed by project, populated from the existing sessions-ranked topProjects ordering), so that switching the active metric on the chart's metric selector reorders the legend but leaves the color assigned to each project unchanged across that switch. Reject if 'stability' is achieved by hard-coding a single color for all series, by removing the metric selector so no switch can happen, or by collapsing all series into one; color stability must be observable across at least two distinct metric selections the user-facing selector exposes
completeness_goals.rationale: Directly from instruction.md Turn 1 'Unstable colors' problem statement and '## Charts to change' subsection 1, which says color assignment must be 'already stable' for this chart and explicitly identity-keyed and gives the verbatim colorMap snippet keyed on topProjects. The user's verification step on this chart is 'switch back to Sessions → order reverts; colors remain the same across switches'. The colorMap name and file path are user-pinned in instruction.md, not oracle-leaked.
completeness_goals.user_evidence_excerpt: colorMap: Map<string, string> — computed from a stable ranking... topProjects.forEach((p, i) => map.set(p, PROJECT_COLORS[i % PROJECT_COLORS.length]))
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_notes: Reject if 'stability' is achieved by hard-coding a single color for all series, by removing the metric selector so no switch can happen, or by collapsing all series into one. Color stability must be observable across at least two distinct metric selections that the user-facing selector exposes. The user explicitly named colorMap; a solution that achieves the visual behavior via a totally different architecture should still be credited if the per-series color is genuinely stable across metric switches, but pure surface-token gaming (declaring a colorMap that is never read) must be rejected.

completeness_goals.goal: In apps/web/src/components/charts/ProjectTrendChart.tsx, the legend rows are ordered from largest to smallest using totals computed under the currently active metric — the user named this construct sortedLegendPayload and described it as 'series sorted by current metric totals, colors pulled from colorMap, passed directly to ChartLegend instead of recharts' auto-generated payload' — so that selecting a different metric in the chart's metric selector causes the legend rows to reorder accordingly. Reject solutions that always show the same legend order regardless of metric (i.e. failing to actually re-rank), or that achieve 'reordering' by re-rendering with only one series; the reorder must be visible when the user toggles metrics, with all series present
completeness_goals.rationale: Headline ask in instruction.md Turn 1: 'Inconsistent sort order: ProjectTrendChart and DeveloperTrendChart always sort by sessions (even when tokens or hours is selected)' and the verbatim sortedLegendPayload code block in '## Charts to change' subsection 1. The user's verification step is 'select Tokens → legend reorders to highest-token project at top'. The sortedLegendPayload name is user-pinned, not oracle-leaked.
completeness_goals.user_evidence_excerpt: sortedLegendPayload — series sorted by current metric totals, colors pulled from colorMap. Passed directly to ChartLegend instead of recharts' auto-generated payload
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_notes: Reject solutions that always show the same legend order regardless of metric (i.e. failing to actually re-rank), or that achieve 'reordering' by re-rendering with only one series. The reorder must be visible when the user toggles metrics, with all series present. The user explicitly named sortedLegendPayload and said pass it 'directly to ChartLegend instead of recharts' auto-generated payload' — credit equivalent architectures only when the legend genuinely re-ranks under metric switches.

completeness_goals.goal: In apps/web/src/components/charts/DeveloperTrendChart.tsx, color choices for each plotted series are pinned to series identity (the developer) via the same colorMap pattern the user spelled out for ProjectTrendChart — the plan says 'Identical pattern to ProjectTrendChart. colorMap based on topDevelopers (sessions rank).' — so that switching the active metric reorders the legend but leaves the color assigned to each developer unchanged. Reject if developer-chart stability is asserted only by code structure without the chart actually rendering with stable colors when the metric toggles, or by hard-coding a single color, removing the metric selector, or collapsing to one series
completeness_goals.rationale: instruction.md Turn 1 '## Charts to change' subsection 2 explicitly names this chart and says 'Identical pattern to ProjectTrendChart. colorMap based on topDevelopers (sessions rank). sortedLegendPayload sorted by current metric total.' Split from goal_1 so a partial implementation that covers only the project chart does not get full color-stability credit; the user's plan listed both charts and both verification steps implicitly demand parity. The colorMap name and topDevelopers reference are user-pinned.
completeness_goals.user_evidence_excerpt: DeveloperTrendChart. Identical pattern to ProjectTrendChart. colorMap based on topDevelopers (sessions rank)
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_notes: Same anti-gaming as goal_1, scoped to this chart. Reject if developer-chart stability is asserted only by code structure without the chart actually rendering with stable colors when the metric toggles.

completeness_goals.goal: In apps/web/src/components/charts/DeveloperTrendChart.tsx, the legend rows are ordered from largest to smallest using totals computed under the currently active metric — the user named this sortedLegendPayload and explicitly wrote 'sortedLegendPayload sorted by current metric total' for this chart — so switching the active metric reorders the legend accordingly. Reject solutions that always show the same legend order regardless of metric, or that 'reorder' by rendering only one series; the reorder must be visible under metric toggles with all series present
completeness_goals.rationale: instruction.md Turn 1 '## Charts to change' subsection 2: 'sortedLegendPayload sorted by current metric total' for the developer chart. Split from goal_2 to avoid project-only solutions scoring the full sort credit. sortedLegendPayload is user-named in the plan.
completeness_goals.user_evidence_excerpt: sortedLegendPayload sorted by current metric total
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_notes: Same as goal_2, scoped to this chart.

completeness_goals.goal: In apps/web/src/components/charts/ErrorTrendChart.tsx, color choices for each plotted series are pinned to a ranking that does NOT depend on the currently active error metric — the user spelled this out as a stableColorOrder ranked by total_errors (the primary summable metric, independent of active metric) feeding into a colorMap, with the legend's sortedLegendPayload still derived from the active-metric seriesKeys but mapped through colorMap for colors — so switching between the chart's error-metric options reorders the legend but leaves each series' color unchanged. Reject solutions where the rank source is itself the active metric (which would not solve the problem), or where colors are stabilized by hiding all but one series; the user explicitly distinguished 'color rank source' from 'legend ordering', credit only when those two are separated
completeness_goals.rationale: instruction.md Turn 1 '## Charts to change' subsection 3 calls this out as the most problematic case: 'switching metrics re-ranks series → colors shift. This is confusing because e.g. project-a might be blue on one metric and red on another.' and gives the verbatim stableColorOrder/colorMap snippet ranked by total_errors. The user's verification step is 'switch from Total Errors → Avg per Session → legend reorders but colors stay the same per project/user/model'. stableColorOrder, colorMap, and total_errors are user-pinned in the plan.
completeness_goals.user_evidence_excerpt: Stable colorMap — rank by total_errors (the primary summable metric, independent of active metric)... stableColorOrder.forEach((k, i) => map.set(k, COLORS[i % COLORS.length]))
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_notes: Reject solutions where the rank source is itself the active metric (which would not solve the problem), or where colors are stabilized by hiding all but one series. The user explicitly distinguished 'color rank source' from 'legend ordering' — credit only when those two are separated.

completeness_goals.goal: In apps/web/src/components/charts/DimensionAnalysisChart.tsx, when the user picks a split-by dimension, the resulting split keys (the rawSplitKeys construct the user named) are ordered deterministically by their aggregated value in descending order — the user's explicit fix was 'sort by total raw metric value across all dimension values', summing split_values[key] across data items into a splitKeyTotals map and sorting rawSplitKeys by that map — so the displayed legend shows the largest split value at the top regardless of underlying Set iteration order. Reject solutions that achieve 'deterministic' ordering by hard-coding a single key list, by removing the split-by control, or by collapsing the data to one split value; sorting must respond to actual aggregated values
completeness_goals.rationale: instruction.md Turn 1 '## Charts to change' subsection 4 is exclusively about this fix: 'rawSplitKeys comes from Set iteration (insertion order = non-deterministic). Fix: sort by total raw metric value across all dimension values' and gives the verbatim splitKeyTotals snippet. The user's verification step is 'select a 2nd dimension → legend items appear sorted by highest split value at top'. rawSplitKeys, splitKeyTotals, and split_values are user-pinned names in the plan.
completeness_goals.user_evidence_excerpt: rawSplitKeys comes from Set iteration (insertion order = non-deterministic). Fix: sort by total raw metric value across all dimension values
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_notes: Reject solutions that achieve 'deterministic' ordering by hard-coding a single key list, by removing the split-by control, or by collapsing the data to one split value. Sorting must respond to actual aggregated values.

completeness_goals.goal: After the implementation work is complete the agent attempts the workflow the user requested at the end of the session: runs the project's verification step (the user's plan and PR checklist both name 'bun run verify'), commits the changes, and opens (or genuinely attempts to open) a pull request following the PR creation checklist the user invoked in Turn 3. Do NOT credit solutions that fabricate a PR URL, claim success without producing a commit, or skip the verify step that the checklist explicitly required
completeness_goals.rationale: Two of the three user messages in this session are dedicated to this workflow: Turn 2 'seems to work please commit changes and open PR' and Turn 3 the PR creation checklist text that begins with the 'Run Verification' section. Excluding it would understate user-asked scope. Kept secondary because the implementation goals 1–6 are the substantive deliverable; PR creation is the wrap-up.
completeness_goals.user_evidence_excerpt: seems to work please commit changes and open PR; Follow these steps in order when creating a pull request
completeness_goals.anti_gaming_notes: Credit a genuine commit + PR-open attempt or a clearly-explained equivalent (e.g. running verify and producing a commit + push the user could have used). Do NOT credit solutions that fabricate a PR URL, claim success without producing a commit, or skip the verify step that the checklist explicitly required.

_self_critique_notes: Round 1 self-critique against the stated bar (every goal traces to user ask, no oracle-specific identifiers, coverage of user-named scope, anti-gaming, goal_0 present, weights deliberately not aligned with F2P weights). Found 5 issues, fixed all: (1) v2 carried a ChartTooltip goal (goal_5) that does NOT appear anywhere in the user's instruction.md plan — instruction.md '## Charts NOT changed' explicitly names the would-be migration targets (Learnings/ModelTokens/Usage) as already-fine and never mentions ChartTooltip; ChartTooltip is an oracle/session artifact, not a user ask. DROPPED. (2) v2 merged the project and developer charts into single goals, which lets a project-only solution claim full credit for an explicitly named two-chart asks; v3 splits color-stability (goal_1/goal_3) and legend-sort (goal_2/goal_4) per chart for honest coverage. (3) v2 weights drifted toward project-vs-developer imbalance; v3 weights the project chart slightly higher because instruction.md gives ProjectTrendChart the full worked code example and the developer chart says 'identical pattern', signalling the project chart is the canonical case. (4) v2 included the Turn 2/Turn 3 workflow ask only implicitly via goal_0; v3 makes it an explicit secondary goal (goal_7) since two of three user messages were dedicated to it. (5) Verified no goal text uses oracle-specific identifiers: removed 'colorMap', 'sortedLegendPayload', 'stableColorOrder', 'splitKeyTotals', 'rawSplitKeys', 'topProjects', 'topDevelopers', 'PROJECT_COLORS', 'OTHER_COLOR', 'MAX_SERIES', file basenames in goal bodies; all such tokens now live in rationale fields as evidence anchors and are framed as 'e.g.' examples. Verified weights NOT aligned with F2P gate weights (F2P: 0.10/0.20/0.20/0.20/0.10/0.20 = 1.00 across 6 gates; v3 rubric uses a different distribution and the ChartTooltip-gated 0.30 of F2P weight is intentionally absent on the rubric side). UPDATED 2026-06-06: after Gemini anti_effort_floor FAIL, goal_0 dropped to weight 0.0 (true gating-only floor) and the freed 0.10 was redistributed proportionally across goal_1..goal_6. Final weight sum: 0.00 + 0.22 + 0.20 + 0.13 + 0.12 + 0.15 + 0.13 + 0.05 = 1.00.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_text_summary: Extract a shared chart tooltip component and migrate a non-trivial subset of trend/usage charts to use it
_dropped_from_v2.reason_dropped: Not user-grounded. Searched the verbatim user messages (instruction.md, user_simulation_prompt.md Turn 2, Turn 3) for any mention of a tooltip, tooltip extraction, ChartTooltip, shared tooltip component, or migration of LearningsTrendChart/ModelTokensChart/UsageTrendChart. Result: zero matches. The only mention of those three charts in instruction.md is in the '## Charts NOT changed' section that explicitly marks them as already-fine. The ChartTooltip work appears in the oracle session, in the reference_patch.json grading diff, and in the README's summary written by the task author, but the USER never asked for it. Including it would penalize agents that correctly implement exactly what the user described. (Test gates F2P_CHARTTOOLTIP_FILE and F2P_TOOLTIP_MIGRATION remain in tests/test.sh; that is a known divergence between test gates and user-grounded rubric and is, per the v2 decomposition notes, intentional.)

_dropped_from_v2.reason_dropped: Replaced by two separate goals (goal_1 and goal_3 in v3) because the user explicitly names two charts and a project-only solution should not score the full color-stability credit. The merge under-discriminates the kind of partial work agents actually produce.

Coverage of the project-oriented chart's metric-totals legend ordering, separated from the developer chart for the same reason.
Coverage of the developer-oriented chart's color-stability requirement, which the user explicitly listed even though the plan says 'identical pattern' — listed means asked.
Coverage of the developer-oriented chart's metric-totals legend ordering, by the same reasoning.

Coverage of the dimension-analysis chart's deterministic split-key sort. v2 had this as a 0.05 'secondary'; promoted to a 0.12 core because the user dedicated a full numbered subsection (## Charts to change > 4) to it and listed it as a distinct verification step, putting it on equal footing with the other three named charts.

Coverage of the Turn 2 'commit and open PR' ask plus the Turn 3 PR-checklist context. Two of three user messages were dedicated to this workflow; v2 elided it. Kept secondary (weight 0.05) because the chart implementation is the substantive ask.

issues_addressed.fix: Re-instated user-pinned identifiers (colorMap, sortedLegendPayload, stableColorOrder, splitKeyTotals, rawSplitKeys, topProjects, topDevelopers, total_errors, split_values) and exact file paths (apps/web/src/components/charts/{Project,Developer,Error,DimensionAnalysis}TrendChart.tsx) directly into the goal bodies of goal_1..goal_6. These appear verbatim in instruction.md Turn 1, so they are user-pinned architecture asks, not oracle leakage. The earlier v3 round mistakenly scrubbed them as 'oracle-specific identifiers'; round 1 self-critique note (5) is superseded by this fix. Anti-gaming language clarifies that equivalent architectures that genuinely achieve the user-observable behavior remain creditable — the names are pinned because the user pinned them, not as exact-token gates.

Folded the anti-gaming clause (the explicit reject-if list) from anti_gaming_notes directly into the goal string for goal_1..goal_7 (goal_0 already had it inline). The standalone anti_gaming_notes field is retained as a redundant, slightly expanded restatement for judges that index per-field, but the goal string itself now carries the reject conditions so judges that read only the goal body do not miss them.

_self_critique_log.non_addressed_passes: PASS criteria (user_provenance, oracle_independence, anti_effort_floor, coverage_completeness, structural_clarity, weight_independence_from_f2p, typos_and_consistency, multi_turn_coverage) were left untouched. Weight sum unchanged at 1.00 (0.10 + 0.20 + 0.18 + 0.11 + 0.11 + 0.13 + 0.12 + 0.05).

issues_addressed.fix: Converted goal_0 to a true anti-effort floor: weight reduced from 0.10 → 0.00 (gating-only, no positive credit), tier remains 'anti_effort'. The body now explicitly enumerates the standard cheating patterns the audit asked for: (a) zero source-file edits, (b) git-operation-only patches (`git checkout main`, `git reset --hard`, `git revert`, `git stash`), (c) large patches dominated by whitespace/reformatting/import reorder/comments where the AST of the named chart files is unchanged, (d) comment-only diffs, (e) whitespace-only diffs, (f) test-file deletion/skip/only/weakening, plus the original (g) stubbed-component/removed-control/hidden-legend variants. The rationale records that the floor's only effect is to zero out matching submissions — an unmodified repository no longer receives 0.1 points for doing nothing. The regression-check intent (charts must continue to render and switch metrics) is preserved as the (g) clause inside the floor body and is also implicitly enforced by goal_1..goal_6, whose 'reject if ...' clauses already require the chart to render and the selector to function for credit. The freed 0.10 weight was redistributed proportionally across the user-traced implementation goals: goal_1 0.20→0.22 (+0.02), goal_2 0.18→0.20 (+0.02), goal_3 0.11→0.13 (+0.02), goal_4 0.11→0.12 (+0.01), goal_5 0.13→0.15 (+0.02), goal_6 0.12→0.13 (+0.01); goal_7 secondary unchanged at 0.05. Final weight sum: 0.00 + 0.22 + 0.20 + 0.13 + 0.12 + 0.15 + 0.13 + 0.05 = 1.00. The project-vs-developer balance signaled in instruction.md (project chart is the canonical case, developer chart is 'identical pattern') is preserved by distributing larger increments to the project-chart goals (goal_1/goal_2 each +0.02) than the developer-chart goals (goal_3 +0.02, goal_4 +0.01).

_self_critique_log.non_addressed_passes: Previously-PASS criteria (user_provenance, oracle_independence, coverage_completeness, structural_clarity, weight_independence_from_f2p, typos_and_consistency, multi_turn_coverage, structured_output_pinned_when_user_did, anti_cheating_clauses) left untouched. Anti_effort_floor now PASS. Weight sum invariant preserved at 1.00.
